Ell PASO. Tex, Nov. 1s.-The sec
ond days session of the twelfth an
nual convention of the National Irri-
cation congress opened with an in
creased attendance.


The days program was full Of
promise, many interesting papers be-
ing scheduled in the several sections.


The subjects for the day were PRO
duction by Irrigation" on which the
principal papers were read by w. J..
Spillman of the department of agri-
culture and Professor Thomas Shaw
of St. Paul, MINI.


In the engineering and mechanics
section W. C. Mendenhall discoursed
interestingly on Underground Wa
tiers of Southern California" giving
practicable ideas on the bringing of
adequate supply to the surface.


Professor A. C. Meade of the Pa
cific coast weather bureau addressed
the "Climatology" section on wet
and Dry Seasons in California and
Arizona" He said in part:


Wet and Dry Seasons.


1.-The country mentioned above has
been somewhat POLITICALLY called THE
Land of Little Rain; but to one
travelling in this section during the
months of August and September of
the curren year the Characterization
given must seem out of keeping with
the general appearance of the soil.
Probably not within recorded history
have such heavy and frequent rains
been reported. Day after day,
throughout the so-called arid region,
especially in the mountains, thunder
CURED. AS there is little or no forest
covering, brush or grass to hold back
the flaw. the runoff was both rapid
and destructive.


Without further delay let us agree
upon the fact that seasons differ, that
there are well marked wet and dry
periods, and that If 1t were possible
to forecast upon a scientific basis,
of course) the general character of
an impending season the achievement
would be one of immeasurable value
to all who live in the southwest.


Causes there must be for these
marked variations in seasons, and
causes which we believe will yet be
understood by man; but not yet are
these known.


I'VE have, however, been able to de-
tect a general relation between the
permanent PRESSURE movements and
the general character of the seasons.
During wet winters the Continental
High il displaced well to the east of
the Rocky mountains, while the North
